ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Singapore roti prata with stuffed fillings. Mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Flat bread) used as the closest defensible category for roti prata. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for stuffed Singapore prata.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for roti prata or Singapore flatbread dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of stuffed prata with chicken luncheon meat, chicken floss, cheese and egg is approximately 160 g, equivalent to one folded piece as served at a Singapore hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Prata (chicken luncheon meat, chicken floss, cheese, egg) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record F05020089 for this exact dish variant. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced from HPB at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from component analysis: cheese (~3.5 g/100 g dish contribution), chicken luncheon meat (~3.0 g/100 g dish contribution), ghee (~2.0 g/100 g dish contribution), totalling approximately 8.5 g/100 g saturated fat. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient data not reported by HPB for this record; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Roti (Indian flatbread, white flour) — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore stuffed prata with chicken luncheon meat, chicken floss, cheese and egg. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white-flour Indian flatbread / roti (GI ~70). The refined wheat flour prata dough is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Protein (18.2 g/100 g) and fat (23.8 g/100 g) from fillings will moderately attenuate glycaemic response, but the low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g) and refined dough base support a high GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 30.7) / 100 ≈ 21.4 for a 160 g serving (carbohydrate_g per serving = 19.16 × 1.6 = 30.66 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 579 mg/100 g from processed chicken luncheon meat, chicken floss, processed cheese and ghee, with a per-serving sodium load of approximately 926 mg for a 160 g portion
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a single 160 g serving delivers approximately 926 mg sodium — exceeding 40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit — and the combination of processed luncheon meat, chicken floss and processed cheese provides no meaningful potassium or fibre offset to mitigate the pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~70) refined wheat flour prata base with low dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g), combined with saturated fat (~8.5 g/100 g) from cheese and luncheon meat that impairs insulin sensitivity, and added sugar in chicken floss
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish or consume only a small portion infrequently; the refined prata dough produces a high glycaemic load (~21 per serving), the saturated fat from cheese and luncheon meat worsens insulin resistance, and the chicken floss contributes additional hidden sugar that further destabilises postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 8.5 g/100 g (exceeding the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold) from cheese, chicken luncheon meat and ghee, combined with high sodium (579 mg/100 g) and ultra-processed meat ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish; the saturated fat content (~8.5 g/100 g) substantially exceeds the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold, the high sodium load elevates blood pressure risk, and the processed meat fillings (luncheon meat, chicken floss) are associated with increased cardiovascular mortality in epidemiological evidence — limit to rare occasional consumption at most.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~8.5 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; processed meat preservatives (sodium nitrates in luncheon meat) and potential trans fat from processed fillings are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the high saturated fat from cheese, luncheon meat and ghee exceeds the RA pro-inflammatory threshold, sodium nitrates in chicken luncheon meat may exacerbate synovial inflammation, and the absence of anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, antioxidants) provides no compensating benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prata with Chicken Luncheon Meat, Chicken Floss, Cheese and Egg is a heavily stuffed variant of Singapore roti prata — a South Indian-origin flatbread that has become a staple of Singapore hawker culture. The prata dough is made from refined wheat flour, water, ghee and salt, stretched thin, folded multiple times to create flaky layers, then pan-fried on a flat griddle with additional ghee until golden and crisp on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. This variant is filled with sliced or diced chicken luncheon meat (a canned processed poultry product), chicken floss (dried shredded seasoned chicken with added sugar and oil), processed cheese slices, and a beaten egg that is cracked directly onto the griddle and folded into the prata. Japanese mayonnaise is typically drizzled inside or on top. The result is a rich, savoury, high-calorie dish with a crispy exterior, creamy and meaty interior, and a complex flavour profile combining the saltiness of luncheon meat, the sweet-savoury character of chicken floss, the mild creaminess of melted cheese, and the richness of egg and ghee. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at 364 kcal/100 g, high in protein (18.2 g/100 g) and fat (23.8 g/100 g, of which approximately 8.5 g is saturated), moderate in carbohydrate (19.16 g/100 g), and high in sodium (579 mg/100 g). Dietary fibre is low at 0.8 g/100 g. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 70 (high) based on the refined flour base. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, low fibre, saturated fat impairing insulin sensitivity), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat exceeding CVD threshold, high sodium, processed meat), or rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory saturated fat and preservatives). It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or working-lunch item at Singapore hawker centres and mamak stalls, and is classified as halal. A typical serving is approximately 160 g (one folded piece), delivering approximately 582 kcal, 29 g protein, 38 g fat, and 926 mg sodium per serving.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record F05020089 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from component analysis of the known fillings. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white-flour Indian flatbread as the reference food. All other micronutrient fields are set to -1 as HPB does not report them for this record.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this record): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record F05020089 does not report detailed fatty acid fractions or micronutrient data for this composite dish. Trans fat is estimated at trace levels from processed fillings but cannot be confirmed from HPB data; set to -1 pending laboratory analysis. Saturated fat of 8.5 g/100 g is a component-based estimate (cheese ~3.5 g, chicken luncheon meat ~3.0 g, ghee ~2.0 g per 100 g of finished dish) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for processed cheese (SR Legacy 01041), canned chicken luncheon meat (Branded Foods), and ghee (SR Legacy 04134). GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white-flour Indian flatbread / roti as the reference food; no direct GI measurement for Singapore stuffed prata with these fillings was found in the University of Sydney database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. Glycemic load per serving (160 g) = (70 × 30.66) / 100 ≈ 21.4. The input record described the dish as containing Japanese mayonnaise; this is reflected in the ingredient list and LLM description but does not materially alter the HPB-reported macronutrient values. Halal classification is based on the use of chicken (not pork) luncheon meat and chicken floss as stated in the dish name and HPB record; consumers should verify individual product halal certification.
